heading: Proemio
card_summary: Chunk 143 comprises the dense scholarly endnotes (pp. 2904–2919) to the third canticle of De Harmonia Mundi, organized under the three "tones" (Tono Primo, Tono Secondo, Tono Terzo). The notes document Zorzi's citations of Vitruvius on bodily proportions, the Sefer Yetzirah's 32 paths via pseudo-Rabad, Timaeus of Locri and Proclus on the world-soul, Avicenna's Canon on the "res non naturales," Boethius De institutione musica on musical therapy (including David's healing of Saul), the Corpus Hermeticum, Plotinus's Enneads (extensively on the virtuous soul's ascent), Agrippa's De occulta philosophia, Ficino's De vita, and Pauline theology (Romans 9 on predestination and election). A notable philological note identifies a reference to the New World discoveries via Pietro Martire d'Anghiera's Decades de orbe novo and Amerigo Vespucci's Mundus novus, inserted into an Origenist context on universal Providence.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 143 (pp. 2904–2919) of the Campanini edition constitutes the apparatus of scholarly footnotes for the *Cantico Terzo* (Third Canticle) of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (1525), subdivided by the text's tripartite musical structure into *Tono Primo*, *Tono Secondo*, and *Tono Terzo*. Rather than original prose by Zorzi, this section is Campanini's editorial apparatus identifying Zorzi's sources, correcting corrupted Latin quotations, and noting intertextual dependencies — itself an extraordinary instrument for understanding the Renaissance Franciscan Kabbalist's syncretistic method.

---

## Tono Primo: Body, Proportion, Medicine, and Music

### Vitruvian Anthropology and Sacred Architecture

The opening notes of *Tono Primo* establish Zorzi's dependence on **Vitruvius, *De architectura* 3.1**, particularly the famous account of the human body as the module of architectural proportion. Campanini notes that notes 1–8 are "citate alla lettera" (literally quoted) from *De architectura* 3.1.2–3, where Vitruvius defines the proportional relationships among bodily members — face, hand, foot, cubit — as the basis of the *homo bene figuratus* inscribed in circle and square. Zorzi grafts this onto **Sefer Yetzirah 5.2** (note 9), linking the Pythagorean-Vitruvian body-canon to the Kabbalistic account of human creation, and then to **Genesis 2:7** (the creation of Adam from dust) and the pseudo-**Timaeus of Locri, *De natura animae et mundi* 223** on the soul's composition.

This is supplemented by **Proclus, *Commentarium in Timaeum* 2.283 and 3.273**, and **Plato, *Timaeus* 36d–e** on the tripartite division of the world-soul. **Calcidius, *Commentarium in Timaeum* 2.167**, whose text Zorzi used in a corrupt form (Campanini corrects *convitium* to *servitium* and *absque agnitione* to *a cognitione revocamur*), mediates Platonic soul-theory. The triad of body–soul–spirit maps onto Noah's ark (*tristegam*, Gen. 6:16), a grecism Zorzi introduced earlier.

### Medicine, Healing, and the Humors

A substantial block of notes concerns Zorzi's medical sources. **Avicenna, *Canon* I, fen 3**, the famous catalogue of *res non naturales* (the six non-natural things that influence health), underlies Zorzi's physiological observations (note 43). **John of Serapion** (*Giovanni Serapione*, not to be confused with John of Damascus) and his *Commentaria de simplicium medicamentorum temperamentis* appear in note 191. The patristic tradition enters via **Jerome, *Epistula* 52.12** (on the body as the soul's tent) and **Sirach 38:1–4** on the physician as instrument of divine healing. **Juvenal, *Satires* 10.356** (*orandum est ut sit mens sana in corpore sano*) is also cited (note 61). The late antique physician **Paul of Aegina** appears unnamed but identified (note 62).

### Music Therapy and Boethius

Notes 183–191 document Zorzi's engagement with **Boethius, *De institutione musica* 1.1** as the principal source for the theory of music's power over the soul and body. Boethius's account of music and medicine is supplemented by **Censorinus, *De die natali* 12** on Pythagoras's use of music (mediated also by **Ficino, *Epistolae* 1.1.91**). The story of **David healing Saul** (1 Sam. 16:16–23) provides the Scriptural anchor for musical therapy. **Thomas Aquinas, *Quaestiones disputatae de virtutibus*** and **Henry of Ghent's *Summa quaestionum ordinariarum*** appear as scholastic mediators of the ethical dimensions of music (notes 192–193).

---

## Tono Secondo: Soul, Election, Gnosis, and the New World

### The Soul's Nature: Aristotle, Plato, and Kabbalah

*Tono Secondo* opens with a concentrated Aristotelian-Platonic cluster. Notes 1–3 cite **Aristotle, *De anima* 2.1 and 2.2** on the soul as form of the body, alongside **Plato, *Leges* 10.892a** on the soul as self-mover. **Proclus, *Comm. in Tim.* 2.103**, the **Pseudo-Pythagorean Carmen aureum 63**, and **Aratus, *Phaenomena* 5** (quoted in **Acts 17:28–29**, "in him we live and move and have our being") together frame the soul's divine origin. **Macrobius, *Commentarium in Somnium Scipionis* 1.14.19** and **Cicerone, *Tusculanae disputationes* 1.19.22** extend the doxography.

A pivotal note (23) identifies a substantial borrowing from **Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* 3.44** — Campanini observes that the preceding passage and much of what follows is "riprese alla lettera" from Agrippa. This is one of the clearest documented cases in the apparatus of Zorzi's dependence on the *De occulta philosophia* (1531/1510), a text whose relationship to the *De Harmonia Mundi* (1525) raises chronological questions Campanini addresses in his introduction.

The **haecceitas** doctrine of **John Duns Scotus** (*Quaestiones in quattuor libros sententiarum* 2, dist. 3, quaest. 7) provides the scholastic ground for individual soul-identity (note 24–25), combined with **Wisdom 8:19** on pre-existent souls and **1 Cor. 12:11** on the Spirit distributing gifts severally. **Origen, *De principiis* 2.9.3–6** underlies the sections on the inequality of rational creatures and their providential ordering (notes 31–36).

### The 32 Paths of the Sefer Yetzirah

Notes 128–140 constitute a dense Kabbalistic excursus. Zorzi's account of the **32 paths of wisdom** (*sentieri*) is sourced to the **pseudo-Rabad commentary on Sefer Yetzirah** (ed. Warsaw 1884, pp. 10a–10b), with Campanini quoting the relevant Hebrew passages directly (in Unicode-corrupted rendering in the digital file). Campanini notes that while Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* also lists the 32 paths, Zorzi does not depend on him — indeed Zorzi's character Simone explicitly states: "Ho ordinato i sentieri a modo mio, mentre altri seguono un ordine diverso." The note exploits the Kabbalistic ambiguity between **berakhah** (blessing) and **berekhah** (pool/cistern) to generate a typological reading. The paths are linked to **Psalm 83:6.8** and **Matthew 5:8** ("Blessed are the pure in heart").

### Romans 9, Predestination, and Universal Providence

A substantial block (notes 54–102) documents Zorzi's engagement with Paul's theology of election in **Romans 9–11**. The apparatus cites Romans 9:11–12 on Jacob and Esau, **Genesis 25:23 and 27:40**, **Malachi 1:2–3** (cited in Rom. 9:13), **Exodus passages on the hardening of Pharaoh's heart** (Ex. 4:21; 7:3; 9:12; 10:20.27; etc.), and Romans 9:17–20 on the potter and the clay. Patristic mediation comes through **Augustine, *De Genesi ad litteram* 11.17**, **Origen, *De principiis* 2.9.4**, and **Jerome's** various commentaries. Zorzi follows Origen's universalist tendency while maintaining Augustinian categories.

### The New World as Providential Problem

One of the most remarkable editorial notes (85) identifies a passage on **peoples without religion** as derived from **Origen, *De principiis* 2.9.5**, but significantly altered by the insertion of a reference to **the Canary Islands and newly discovered peoples**:

> "Il passo, fatta eccezione per il riferimento alle Canarie, evidentemente influenzato dalla recente letteratura sul cannibalismo caraibico, proviene da Origene, *De principiis* 2.9.5."

Note 89 extends this, with Campanini identifying a reference to **Pietro Martire d'Anghiera's *Decades de orbe novo*** and **Amerigo Vespucci's *Mundus novus*** (Augsburg 1504), quoting Vespucci's description of the indigenous peoples:

> "Vivunt secundum naturam et epicurei potius dici possunt quam stoici."
> <cite>Amerigo Vespucci, *Mundus novus*, ed. Augsburg 1504, p. [11a]</cite>

Campanini suggests Zorzi is referring to the Americas rather than the Philippines or Moluccas, placing this within the broader Renaissance problem of providence and salvation for peoples unknown to Scripture. The terminology (confusing east and west) derives from Martire d'Anghiera's accounts.

### Corpus Hermeticum, Averroes, and the Mind

Notes 174–177 cite **Corpus Hermeticum 4.3–6** on the two paths of knowledge, the mind (nous) given to some souls and withheld from others — directly relevant to Zorzi's Kabbalistic-Hermetic synthesis on intellect and election. Campanini notes that where Zorzi writes "terra" the original CH has "cielo" (note 174). **Averroes** (unnamed, identified in note 172 as "com'è ovvio") provides the Aristotelian counterpoint.

### The Zohar

Note 189 identifies a reference to the **Zohar** — specifically "la prima parte del terzo volume dello Zohar nelle edizioni correnti" — and directs readers to Campanini's introduction for a full discussion of the extent of Zorzi's knowledge of the Zohar during the composition of the *De Harmonia Mundi*.

---

## Tono Terzo: Angels, Spiritual Ascent, Resurrection, and Demonology

### Angelic Hierarchy and the Patriarchs

*Tono Terzo* opens with **1 Cor. 15:53** on the resurrection of the body and **1 Cor. 10:4** on the spiritual rock. Note 6 provides a detailed Kabbalistic note: Zorzi assigns guardian angels to the patriarchs following **pseudo-Rabad's preface to the Sefer Yetzirah**, also found in **Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica***. Campanini corrects Zorzi's assignment: in the source, Jacob's angel is **Peli'el**, while Isaac's is **Rafa'el** — not the same angel as Zorzi claims. The **Legenda aurea** of **Jacopo da Varagine** supplies hagiographic material on angelic apparitions (notes 9–10), including the bilocations of **Ambrose** (note 237 in Tono Secondo) and the ecstasies of **Francis of Assisi** (*Legenda maior* 4.10, represented by Giotto, note 238).

### Apocalyptic Cosmology and Fall of Satan

Notes 39–52 document the heavily apocalyptic texture of *Tono Terzo*, with dense citation of **Apocalypse 12** (the Woman clothed with the sun, the dragon, Michael's war) alongside **Psalm 2:7–9** on the messianic Son. **Anselm of Canterbury, *Cur Deus homo* 1.18** provides the Anselmian satisfaction theory as backdrop to Zorzi's angelic Fall cosmology. **Aristotle, *Metaphysics* 12.8.1074a** (55 celestial spheres) is noted with the correction that this expands Eudoxus's 26 and Callippus's 33 spheres (note 54).

### Demonic Temptation and Free Will

Notes 70–82 draw extensively on **Origen, *De principiis* 3.2.3–4** on demonic temptation, **Sirach 15:14.18** on free will, and a proverbial maxim (*"Antiquus hostis, sicut contra consentientes fortis est, ita contra resistentes debilis"*) that Campanini traces to **Gregory the Great, *Moralia in Hiob* 22.43**, though Zorzi attributes it variously to Jerome, Gregory, and Bernard across different works (note 74). The Dialogue of Muhammad and Abdullah ibn Salam (from the **Collectio toletana**, translated by **Hermann the Dalmatian**) also appears (note 59), indicating Zorzi's awareness of Islamic angelology.

### Plotinus on Virtuous Ascent

The closing notes of *Tono Secondo* (notes 211–230) constitute a sustained Plotinian sequence: **Plotinus, *Enneads* 1.2** (*On Virtues*) dominates, with Campanini citing *Enn.* 1.2.5, 1.2.6–7, 4.7.1, and 1.1.8 on the soul's assimilation to God, homoiosis theoi, read through **John 1:12** ("to those who received him he gave power to become children of God"), **John 16:28**, and **1 Cor. 6:17** ("he who is joined to the Lord is one spirit"). **Proclus, *Comm. in Tim.* 1.211** and **Macrobius, *Comm. in somn. Scip.* 1.8** mediate the Neoplatonic virtue-hierarchy. The synthesis is Christological: the Plotinian ascent is mapped onto Pauline and Johannine union with the Logos.

---

## Editorial and Philological Observations

Campanini's apparatus throughout this chunk performs substantive philological work: it corrects corruptions in Zorzi's Latin text of Calcidius (notes 18–20), identifies Zorzi's practice of citing from memory (note 181, on Jerome *Epist.* 53.3), tracks mediation through Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* (notes 23, 94, 128), Ficino's *De vita* (notes 113–116, 131–132), and Ficino's *Epistolae* (note 187). The note on the pseudo-Rabad's Hebrew text of the 32 paths (notes 134–140) offers the Hebrew citations with diplomatic precision despite encoding corruption in the digital file.

The apparatus also flags Zorzi's geographic knowledge of the New World (notes 85, 89), his engagement with Islamic sources via the Collectio toletana (note 59), and his use of the Zohar (note 189) — collectively establishing the *De Harmonia Mundi* as a text at the intersection of Christian Kabbalah, Renaissance Neoplatonism, Hermetism, scholastic theology, humanist philology, and early modern geographic horizons.
